module: FA COPD and Asthma Part I

questions.question: Airway narrowing and obstruction in patients with COPD can be caused by:
questions.explanation: The cardinal pathophysiologic feature of COPD is airflow limitation caused by airway narrowing and/or obstruction, loss of elastic recoil, or both. Airway narrowing and obstruction result from a combination of mechanisms, including inflammation-mediated mucus hypersecretion, mucus plugging, mucosal oedema, bronchospasm, peribronchial fibrosis, and remodelling of the small airways - so all of the listed mechanisms contribute.

questions.question: The work of breathing (WOB) is often found to be increased in COPD patients. Reasons for such increase in WOB are:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Increased pressure necessary to overcome increased airway resistance

statements.label: B
statements.text: Altered geometry of diaphragm (flattened and shortened)

statements.label: C
statements.text: Disturbed energy metabolism in the respiratory muscles as a consequence of hypoxaemia and acidosis

statements.label: D
statements.text: Increased muscle mass as a consequence of increased breathing efforts

questions.explanation: During an acute exacerbation of COPD, the respiratory muscles face an increased resistive and elastic load (including dynamic hyperinflation/PEEPi), which raises the pressure needed to breathe; diaphragmatic geometry is also altered, and hypoxaemia/acidosis disturb respiratory muscle energy metabolism. Increased breathing effort does not produce increased respiratory muscle mass acutely - if anything, respiratory muscle dysfunction and fatigue are the concern.

questions.question: Your patient, a 19-year-old athlete with newly diagnosed asthma, presents at your clinic for patient education regarding situations that could precipitate an asthma attack. Which of the following precipitants may be associated with an asthma attack?

statements.label: A
statements.text: Exercise

statements.label: B
statements.text: Allergens

statements.label: C
statements.text: Pollutants

statements.label: D
statements.text: Warm weather

questions.explanation: Recognised precipitants of an asthma attack include allergens (e.g. pollens, moulds), airway irritants and pollutants (e.g. chemical fumes, cigarette smoke, automobile exhaust), cold air, and exercise. Warm weather itself is not a typical trigger - cold air is.

questions.question: Which of the following are the pathogenetic factors for airway obstruction in acute exacerbation of asthma?

statements.label: A
statements.text: Smooth muscle hypertrophy

statements.label: B
statements.text: Chronic bronchoconstriction

statements.label: C
statements.text: Acute bronchoconstriction

statements.label: D
statements.text: Airway oedema

questions.explanation: The factors involved in airflow obstruction during an acute asthma exacerbation are acute bronchoconstriction, airway oedema, and chronic mucus plug formation, together with airway remodelling over time. Smooth muscle hypertrophy and chronic (rather than acute) bronchoconstriction are features of longer-term airway remodelling, not the acute pathogenetic mechanism being asked about here.

questions.question: Which are the main components in the pathophysiology of asthma?

statements.label: A
statements.text: Airway inflammation

statements.label: B
statements.text: Airway inflation

statements.label: C
statements.text: Intermittent airflow obstruction

statements.label: D
statements.text: Bronchial hyperresponsiveness

questions.explanation: The pathophysiology of asthma is complex, involving airway inflammation, bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and intermittent airflow obstruction. 'Airway inflation' is not a recognised component of asthma pathophysiology - only airway inflammation is.

questions.question: Hypoxia in patients with COPD is mainly due to:
questions.explanation: Hypoxaemia is a cardinal feature of acute exacerbations of both COPD and asthma and is mainly caused by ventilation/perfusion (V/Q) mismatching. Alveolar hypoventilation, shunt, and diffusion impairment can all contribute to hypoxaemia in other contexts, but V/Q mismatch is the dominant mechanism in COPD.

questions.question: Which of the following are associated with lung hyperinflation-induced diaphragmatic dysfunction in COPD patients?

statements.label: A
statements.text: Worsening in length-tension relationship due to the increase in the zone of apposition and increase of diaphragmatic curvature

statements.label: B
statements.text: The decrease in the elastic recoil of the thoracic cage

statements.label: C
statements.text: The increase in the elastic recoil of the thoracic cage

statements.label: D
statements.text: The change in mechanical arrangement of costal and crural components of the diaphragm

questions.explanation: Lung hyperinflation places the diaphragm at a mechanical disadvantage through a worsening length-tension relationship caused by a decrease (not increase) in the zone of apposition and a reduction (not increase) in diaphragmatic curvature, together with an increase (not decrease) in the elastic recoil of the thoracic cage, and a change in the mechanical arrangement of the costal and crural diaphragmatic components. Statements A and B reverse the actual direction of these changes.

questions.question: Hyperinflation of the lung is a common pathophysiologic feature in both COPD and asthma. The pathophysiology underlying hyperinflation includes:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Increased airway resistance resulting in dynamic hyperinflation during expiration

statements.label: B
statements.text: Hypoxic hyperventilation with increased functional residual capacity (FRC)

statements.label: C
statements.text: Destruction of lung parenchyma resulting in a decrease in pulmonary elastic recoil forces

statements.label: D
statements.text: Airway collapse with trapping of air distal to the point of airflow cessation (equal pressure point) in the airway

questions.explanation: Dynamic hyperinflation results from high expiratory airway resistance combined with expiratory flow limitation, reduced elastic recoil (as in emphysema), high ventilatory demands, and a short expiratory time relative to the time constant of the lung - reflected in airway collapse and gas trapping distal to the equal pressure point. Hyperventilation driven by hypoxia is not the mechanism linking these processes to a rising FRC.

questions.question: Which immune cells are not identified in airway inflammation in asthma?
questions.explanation: Cell types identified in airway inflammation in asthma include eosinophils, mast cells, basophils, epithelial cells, macrophages, and activated T-lymphocytes. B-lymphocytes are not among the cell types classically implicated in the local airway inflammatory infiltrate of asthma.
